title: Creation
description: Genesis 1-2 opens with two accounts of the world's origins, describing the ordered emergence of the cosmos, life, and humanity as the deliberate work of God. Read within the Bible's own literary and theological context, these chapters are generally treated as a theological account of purpose and relationship rather than a scientific or strictly chronological record.

title: The Age of the Patriarchs
description: Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob are described as founding figures of Israel, migrating through Canaan and Egypt. Genesis presents this period through family narratives rather than official records.

title: The Exodus from Egypt
description: The book of Exodus describes Moses leading enslaved Israelites out of Egypt toward Canaan, an event that becomes the central memory of deliverance referenced throughout the rest of the Hebrew Bible.

title: Judges and Settlement
description: After entering Canaan, Israel is described as a loose confederation of tribes led by intermittent local leaders called judges, without a central king or capital.

title: The United Kingdom
description: Saul, David, and Solomon are described as the first kings of a unified Israel. David establishes Jerusalem as his capital; Solomon builds the first Temple there.

title: Division of the Kingdom
description: After Solomon's death, the kingdom splits into Israel in the north and Judah in the south, beginning centuries of separate, often conflicting, political histories.

title: Fall of the Northern Kingdom
description: The Assyrian Empire conquers the northern kingdom of Israel and disperses much of its population, an event referenced by later prophets as a warning.

title: Babylonian Exile
description: Babylon destroys Jerusalem and its Temple, exiling much of Judah's population. This trauma reshapes Jewish religious life and produces much of the Bible's poetry of lament and hope, including Jeremiah's letters to the exiles.

title: Return from Exile
description: After Persia conquers Babylon, King Cyrus permits exiled Jews to return home and rebuild the Temple, an event described in Ezra and Nehemiah.

title: Birth of Jesus
description: The Gospels of Matthew and Luke describe Jesus's birth in Bethlehem during the reign of Herod the Great, under Roman occupation of Judea.

title: Ministry of Jesus
description: The Gospels describe roughly three years of public teaching, centered in Galilee and Jerusalem, ending with his crucifixion under the Roman governor Pontius Pilate.

title: Early Church Begins
description: The book of Acts describes the emergence of the earliest Christian community in Jerusalem shortly after Jesus's death, initially made up entirely of Jewish followers.

title: Paul's Conversion
description: Saul of Tarsus, previously a persecutor of Christians, describes a dramatic change of course on the road to Damascus, becoming the apostle Paul and a central figure in early Christian expansion.

title: Paul's Missionary Journeys
description: Paul travels extensively through Asia Minor and Greece, founding churches in cities like Ephesus and Corinth, and writing letters that later form a large portion of the New Testament.

title: Destruction of the Second Temple
description: Rome destroys Jerusalem and its Temple after a Jewish revolt, an event that permanently reshaped Jewish religious practice and is referenced as a turning point in early Christian writings.
